# Project Charta
## <span style="color: #009a27;">Context and Scope</span>
Business travel is common in many companies. Employees travel for meetings, conferences, or to work with colleagues in other locations. However, business travel, especially flights, also produces CO₂ emissions, which has an impact on the environment.

In this project, we will analyse and visualize a dataset about business travel. The dataset includes information about transport mode, travel distance, travel purpose, and estimated CO₂ emissions.
The dataset covers business trips from 2017 to early 2026 and represents travel activities within one company with several business units.

The goal of this project is to create an interactive dashboard that helps users understand:

- how travel behaviour changes over time
- why people travel (travel purpose)
- how transport choices affect CO₂ emissions

The dashboard should help answer questions such as:

- How have travel emissions changed over time?
- Which transport modes produce the most CO₂ emissions?
- Which travel purposes (e.g. meetings, conferences) occur most often and contribute most to CO₂ emissions?
- How do different travel options compare in terms of CO₂ emissions and travel duration?

The dashboard should also support simple decisions by allowing users to compare transport modes and understand how much CO₂ different trips produce.
In addition, the dashboard helps users understand which travel purposes cause high CO₂ emissions. This can support reflection on whether certain trips are necessary or if alternative solutions, such as online meetings, could be considered.

The visualization product will be available on a website.

## <span style="color: #009a27;">Project Objectives and Success Criteria</span>
From a stakeholder perspective, the project aims to:

- Provide a clear overview of business travel and CO₂ emissions
- Show how emissions develop over time
- Identify which travel purposes generate the most emissions
- Compare transport modes and their environmental impact
- Support users in understanding and comparing travel options

The final result will be an interactive dashboard that allows users to explore the dataset


Qualitative success criteria:

- The dashboard allows users to quickly understand travel emissions and patterns without needing manual data preparation
- Users can identify trends over time and detect changes in travel behaviour (e.g. increase or decrease of emissions).
- Users can clearly see which travel purposes cause the most CO₂ emissions and reflect on whether these trips are necessary.
- The dashboard supports users in communicating insights (e.g. for reports or presentations) using clear and simple visualizations.

Quantitative success criteria:

- Users are able to identify key trends (e.g. increase or decrease in emissions) within 30–60 seconds.
- Users can filter the data by at least two attributes (e.g., year, transport mode).
- Test users can identify at least two insights about travel patterns within one minute of interacting with the dashboard.


*Out of Scope:* The project will not include recommendations for specific travel options or policies. 

## <span style="color: #009a27;">Stakeholder Analysis</span>

| Stakeholder | Project Role | Goals and Interests | Power | Interest |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Laura Smith (Sustainability Manager / SM)** | Primary User | Needs accurate data to monitor corporate CO₂ targets, generate annual reports, and justify new sustainable travel policies. | High | High |
| **Alex Hauser (Head of HR / HR)** | Primary User | Holds formal authority over the company's travel policy. Sets CO₂ budgets per subunit, decides on permitted transport modes, and enforces compliance. Needs a clear, up-to-date overview to make timely and justifiable policy decisions. | High | High |
| **Business Unit Manager** | Key User | Wants to balance financial travel budgets with allocated CO₂ limits while maintaining team productivity. | High | High |
| **Management** | Project Sponsors | Focused on overarching corporate goals, achieving ESG mandates, and reducing overall business costs. | High | Medium |
| **Employees**| Affected Group | Directly impacted by travel policies derived from the dashboard. Interested in transparency and efficient routes. | Low | High |
| **IT / Web-Administration** | Support and Infrastructure | Responsible for the technical integration of the dashboard into the company’s website. Focused on security and stability. | High | Low |
| **Project Team** | Creators and Developers | Goal is to successfully clean the data, build an intuitive dashboard, and meet the project requirements. | Medium | High |
| **Travel Agency** | Data Provider (External) | Processes bookings and provides raw data. No direct interest in the internal visualization. | Low | Low |

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {"theme": "base", "themeVariables": {"quadrant1Fill": "#b1facc", "quadrant2Fill": "#bbdcff", "quadrant3Fill": "#ffb9b9" , "quadrant4Fill": "#ffffb7"}}}%%
quadrantChart
    title Stakeholder Matrix
    x-axis "Low interest" --> "High interest"
    y-axis "Low Power" --> "High Power"

    "SM": [0.95, 0.85]
    "Management": [0.52, 0.90]
    "Project Team": [0.75, 0.50]
    "IT / Web-Admin": [0.25, 0.90]
    "Employee": [0.80, 0.10]
    "Travel Agency": [0.10, 0.10]
    "HR": [0.90, 0.80]

```


| Quadrant | Category | Strategy | Stakeholders | Consequences |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| <span style="color: #b1facc;">**Green**</span> | High / High | **Manage Closely** | SM, Management, HR | They decide on the project's success and have strong interest. They must be actively involved in decision-making. |
| <span style="color: #bbdcff;">**Blue**</span> | High / Low | **Keep Satisfied** | IT / Web-Admin | Have the power but little content-related interest. You must meet their requirements. |
| <span style="color: #f0f01b;">**Yellow**</span> | Low / High | **Keep Informed** | Project Team, Employee | Highly affected by the project but have little decision-making power.|
| <span style="color: #ffb9b9;">**Red**</span> | Low / Low | **Monitor** | Travel Agency | Neither high influence nor strong interest in the project itself.|


## <span style="color: #009a27;">User Analysis</span>

### <span style="color: #009a27;">**Persona 1: Head of HR**</span>
![](../charta-pictures/alex_hauser.png)

| | |
|---|---|
|**Name**| Alex Hauser|
|**Age**| 48|
|**Role**| Head of HR|
|**Nationality**| Swiss|
|**Degree**| Master's in Human Resources Management|
|**Domain Expertise**| High (expert in HR policy, organizational governance, and compliance)|
|**Data Literacy**| Low to Medium (comfortable reading dashboards and KPIs, but not a data analyst — relies on clear visuals rather than raw numbers)|
|**Technical Environment**| Desktop computer at the office, occasionally tablet in meetings.|
|**Frequency of Use**| Monthly (for policy reviews and budget decisions), ad hoc when a specific subunit requires intervention|

Alex Hauser grew up near Basel and has lived in the Zurich area for the past fifteen years. He studied HR Management and has spent his entire career in human resources, working his way up from HR Business Partner to his current role as Head of HR at the company, where he has been for six years. He is known for being decisive, fair, and politically savvy — he understands how to get things done in a large organization without creating unnecessary conflict.

Outside of work, Alex is an avid hiker and spends most weekends in the mountains with his partner. He is practical by nature and has little patience for complexity that does not add value. He trusts data when it is presented clearly, but switches off quickly when faced with charts that require too much interpretation.

In his role, Alex holds the formal authority to set and enforce the company's travel policy. This means he decides which transport modes are permitted for which types of trips, sets annual CO₂ budgets per business unit, and determines consequences for subunits that consistently exceed their limits. He does not do the analysis himself — that is Laura's job — but he is the one who turns insights into binding decisions. He works closely with the Sustainability Manager and receives regular briefings from her, but when it comes to actually changing the policy, the decision is his.

He is under pressure from the executive board to demonstrate measurable progress on the company's ESG (Environmental, Social, and Governance) commitments, while also keeping travel costs under control. These two goals are not always easy to reconcile, and he needs clear, trustworthy data to justify the trade-offs he makes.

**User Tasks:**

- Review CO₂ budget status per subunit for the current year and identify which units are at risk
- Monitor overall travel costs to ensure spending stays within approved limits
- Assess travel purpose distribution to spot patterns that may indicate policy misuse
- Compare transport options (CO₂ and cost) when considering updates to the travel policy
- Make binding decisions on permitted transport modes, budget limits, and policy enforcement

**Pains:**

- **No single overview:** Without a central tool, getting a clear picture of where each subunit stands requires chasing reports from multiple people — time he does not have.
- **Conflicting pressures:** The board wants lower CO₂ and lower costs, but these do not always point in the same direction. He needs data to justify the trade-offs publicly.
- **Delayed information:** By the time summary reports reach him, decisions have already been made at the employee level and are difficult to reverse.
- **Resistance to policy change:** Business unit managers push back on new travel restrictions. He needs clear, undeniable evidence to hold his ground.

**Gains:**

- **Immediate budget clarity:** A dashboard that shows him within seconds which subunits are on track and which are not, without needing to ask anyone.
- **Justifiable decisions:** Clear transport option comparisons that give him a solid, data-backed reason for why a policy change is necessary.
- **Cost control at a glance:** A simple cost overview so he can flag overspending early and act before it becomes a problem.
- **Confidence:** Knowing the data he is basing his decisions on is current, accurate, and easy to explain to others.

#### <span style="color: #59d177;">**Use Case Scenario: Head of HR**</span>

**Context:**

It is mid-year and Alex is preparing for his quarterly meeting with the executive board. One of the agenda items is the company's progress against its annual CO₂ travel budget. He needs to know which business units are on track and whether any immediate policy action is required.

**The Action:**

Alex opens the Decision Overview page of the dashboard. He uses the subunit filter to check each unit's CO₂ budget status and immediately notices that one subunit has already used 78% of its annual allocation by July — well ahead of pace. He checks the travel purpose breakdown for that subunit and sees that a disproportionate share of trips are categorized as internal meetings. He then glances at the travel cost summary and notices that this subunit is also among the highest spenders — further reinforcing that something needs to change.
**The Insight (How the dashboard helps):**

- **Budget at a glance: The CO₂ budget visualization immediately shows him which subunits are on track and which are at risk — no manual calculation or data request needed.
- **Travel purpose check: The travel purpose breakdown reveals that the over-budget subunit's emissions are largely driven by internal meetings — a category where remote alternatives are readily available and no transport comparison is needed.
- **Cost confirmation: The travel cost summary shows that the same subunit is also one of the highest spenders, making the case for intervention clear from both an environmental and a financial perspective.

**The Outcome:**

Based on what he sees, Alex makes an immediate policy decision: all internal meetings for that subunit must be held remotely for the remainder of the year, with no exceptions unless approved by him personally. He communicates this directly to the relevant business unit manager before the board meeting. The dashboard gave him everything he needed to act in under ten minutes — no reports requested, no analysis delegated, no delays.

### <span style="color: #009a27;">**Persona 2: Sustainability Manager**</span>
![](../charta-pictures/laura_smith.png)

| | |
|---|---|   
|**Name**| Laura Smith|
|**Age**| 32|
|**Role**| Sustainability Manager|
|**Nationality**| British|
|**Degree**| Master’s in Environmental Science|
|**Domain Expertise**| High (expert in sustainability and environmental impact)|
|**Data Literacy**| Medium (comfortable with data but not an expert)|
|**Technical Environment**| Desktop computer with a large monitor for analysis, but manages everything via cloud tools on her tablet and phone while agile working.|
|**Frequency of Use**| Daily (for monitoring and reporting purposes)|

Laura lifes in a minimalistic, shared apartment in Zurich-West, filled with upcycled furniture and numerous indoor plants. She commutes daily by bicycle or train, valuing the physical activity and the emission consequences travel. Sundays are spent hiking in the Alps, combining her love for nature with physical challenge. She speaks fluent English and professional German. Curreently she is trying to learn some basic Swiss German to better connect with her local colleagues and stakeholders.

She joined the company 1.5 years ago as a Senior Sustainability Analyst and was promoted to Manager 4 months ago due to her ambition and early successes in automating initial reporting workflows. She expects tools to work instantly. A clunky, buggy software experience causes her immediate frustration. Her philosophy is: "Let the data flow, then let the analysis begin." 

As a digital native, Laura treats data as a product. She doesn't just analyze it, she expects it to be auditable, scalable, and beautifully visual. She is passionate about using data to drive real change in corporate sustainability and is always looking for ways to make complex information more accessible and actionable for her colleagues and senior leadership. 

Her main goal is to reduce the company's carbon footprint from business travel and to communicate the company's sustainability efforts effectively to stakeholders. She is also focused on identifying trends in travel behaviour and emissions to inform future strategies and policies. Laura is under pressure to deliver quick results due to new regulations and corporate commitments, but she is determined to use data-driven insights to make a meaningful impact on the company's sustainability performance.

**User Tasks:**

- Monitor and report on the company's CO₂ emissions from business travel
- Identify trends and patterns in travel behaviour and emissions
- prepare reports and presentations for senior management and stakeholders
- Convince skeptical senior leadership of sustainable investments using hard financial and environmental data.

**Pains:**

- **Data Mess:** Spends 70% of her time chasing incomplete Excel sheets from different departments instead of analyzing data.
- **Resistance:** Faced with "We’ve always done it this way" from older managers when she proposes train travel over short-haul flights.
- **Pressure:** The board expects instant, ESG results due to new regulations.
- **Difficulty:** accessing and analyzing travel data from multiple sources
- **Time-consuming:** process to create reports and visualizations manually

**Gains:**

- **A "Single Source of Truth":** An interactive dashboard where all travel data flows together automatically and cleanly.
- **Automated Reporting:** Being able to generate the annual travel emission report with three clicks, saving days of manual work.
- **Visual Power:** Professional, undeniable visualizations that make the argument for a "Train First" policy obvious to anyone.
- **Ability to quickly identify and analyze** travel patterns and emissions
- **Improved decision-making** capabilities based on data-driven insights
- **Enhanced communication** of sustainability efforts to stakeholders through clear visualizations

#### <span style="color: #59d177;">**Use Case Scenario: Sustainability Manager**</span>

**Context:**

It is the end of the year, and Laura needs to prepare the annual sustainability report for the executive board. She has to present the overall trends in business travel emissions from the past few years and propose new emission reduction strategies for the upcoming year.

**The Action:**

Instead of spending days manually collecting Excel sheets from different departments and building complex pivot tables, Laura opens the Analysis & Reports page of the Business Travel Dashboard on her desktop monitor. She uses the year and subunit filters to explore the company's total carbon footprint across different dimensions.

**The Insight (How the dashboard helps):**

- **Long-term trend at a glance:** The cumulative CO₂ emissions line chart immediately shows her how the company's total emissions have developed since 2017 — including the visible dip during the pandemic years and the recovery afterwards.
- **Transport mode breakdown:** The CO₂ emissions by transport mode chart reveals that flights account for the vast majority of emissions despite being used less frequently than trains — a striking insight that is easy to communicate to the board.
- **Budget compliance overview:** The CO₂ budget limit chart shows her at a glance which subunits have consistently used a high proportion of their annual budget, allowing her to identify where policy interventions are most needed.
- **Travel purpose detail:** The detailed travel purpose breakdown (showing both percentage and absolute counts) helps her identify which trip categories are driving emissions and whether those trips could be substituted with remote alternatives.
- **Single-year view:** She switches to the single-year CO₂ chart to show how emissions accumulated month by month against the budget threshold — a compelling visual for demonstrating near-limit situations to senior leadership.

**The Outcome:**

Laura exports the charts she needs directly from the dashboard and pastes them into her board presentation. The dashboard saved her days of manual data preparation. In the board meeting, she confidently uses the transport mode breakdown and budget compliance charts to argue for a strengthened train-first policy for all routes under 400 km, backed by clear and undeniable visual evidence.


## <span style="color: #009a27;">Situation Assessment</span>
Dataset:

The dataset contains approcimately 25,000 business travel recorde with variables such as:

- `date` - of travel
- `transport_mode` - transport mode (e.g., flight, train, rental car, etc.)
- `km` - travel distance 
- `CO2e` - estimated CO₂ emissions
- `travel_purpose` - reason for the travel

Personnel:

- Jasmin Fankhauser
- Séverine Stadler
- Lia Sifrig
- Anna Kaufmann

Material: 

- Personal laptops

software/tools:

- Python for data exploration
- Streamlit for visualization / dashboard development
- Gemini + ChatGPT for creating pictures (Personas) and corrections 

Constraints and risks:

- Limited time for data exploration and visualization development
- Possible missing or incomplete data in the dataset
- Learning new tool for dashboard development (Streamlit) takes time

## <span style="color: #009a27;">Visualization Concept</span>
The project will create a focused interactive dashboard with two separate pages, each tailored to a specific user role. Users can navigate freely between the two pages using a top-level navigation menu.

**Product form**: 

The dashboard consists of two role-based pages — a Decision Overview page for the Head of HR and an Analysis & Reports page for the Sustainability Manager. Both pages are web-based and built with Streamlit. It is assumed that the travel options data is maintained and updated regularly by a responsible person within the company. In a real-world implementation, views could additionally be role-restricted by login.

**Page 1 — Decision Overview (Head of HR):**

This page is designed for quick, operational decision-making based on current-year data. It includes a subunit filter and is fixed to the current year only, keeping the view focused and uncluttered.

- A CO₂ budget gauge or progress bar per subunit showing how much of the annual CO₂ allocation has already been used (`CO2e`, subunit). This gives an immediate sense of budget status at a glance.
- A travel cost summary displaying key cost figures for the current year, allowing the Head of HR to monitor spending across subunits.
- A travel purpose pie chart showing the percentage breakdown of trips by purpose (`travel_purpose`). This helps identify whether trips are distributed as expected or whether certain categories are unexpectedly dominant.
- A travel options comparison table showing available transport options for common routes (departure, destination, CO₂ emissions, cost), supporting informed policy decisions about which modes to recommend or restrict.

**Page 2 — Analysis & Reports (Sustainability Manager):**

This page supports in-depth exploration and reporting. It includes filters for year and subunit — applicable to all charts except where noted — and all charts are exportable for use in reports and presentations.

- A cumulative CO₂ emissions line chart showing total emissions summed over all years (`date`, `CO2e`). This allows users to see the long-term progression of emissions, including the effect of external events such as the COVID-19 pandemic. Filterable by subunit.
- A single-year CO₂ emissions line chart (Jan–Dec) with a horizontal budget limit line, showing how emissions accumulated over the course of one year relative to the annual threshold (`CO2e`, budget limit). Filterable by year and subunit.
- A CO₂ emissions by transport mode chart showing the total emissions contribution of each transport mode (`transport_mode`, `CO2e`). This helps identify which modes drive the most emissions, even if they are used less frequently. Filterable by year and subunit.
- Key performance indicators (KPIs) showing total number of trips, number of flights, and number of train journeys. Filterable by year and subunit.
- A detailed travel purpose breakdown showing both percentage share and absolute counts per purpose (`travel_purpose`), giving more granular insight than the simplified view on Page 1. Filterable by year and subunit.
- A CO₂ budget limit chart showing, per subunit and per year, how much of the annual budget was used (e.g. 50% in 2017, 80% in 2018). This makes it easy to spot trends in budget compliance across subunits over time.
- A raw data table providing access to the underlying dataset for further manual analysis or verification.
- Export functionality allowing users to save individual charts for use in reports and presentations.

Tooltips will show additional details on all charts where relevant.

**Interactivity**:

Page 1 (Decision Overview) includes a subunit filter and is fixed to the current year. Page 2 (Analysis & Reports) includes filters for year and subunit, applicable across all charts. Both pages include tooltips for additional detail. Page 2 additionally supports chart export for reporting purposes. These interactive features allow each user to focus on what is relevant to their role without being overwhelmed by unnecessary complexity.

**Narrative and annotation**: 

Each chart will have a descriptive title and clear axis labels so that the meaning is easy to interpret without prior technical knowledge. Short annotations may highlight important patterns, such as a subunit approaching its CO₂ budget limit or a notable shift in transport mode usage over time. These guide users through the dashboard and help them draw conclusions quickly.

**Target medium and integration**: 

The dashboard will be presented as a web-based prototype using Streamlit, viewable in a browser on a laptop or desktop computer. It is primarily intended for use within the course and for presentation purposes. In a real-world scenario, a similar dashboard could be integrated into a company intranet or sustainability reporting system.

**Cognitive and analytical value**: 

The two-page structure reduces cognitive load by ensuring each user only sees what is relevant to their role. The Decision Overview page allows the Head of HR to assess the situation and act quickly. The Analysis & Reports page supports deeper pattern recognition over time — identifying emission trends, budget compliance issues, and the environmental impact of different transport modes. Together the pages cover both operational and strategic information needs.

**Communicative value**: 

The dashboard presents key information clearly without interpreting or evaluating the results. It is designed as an exploratory tool that allows users to analyse the data and draw their own conclusions. The export functionality specifically supports the communicative needs of the Sustainability Manager, who regularly needs to present findings to senior leadership.

**Experiential and aesthetic value**: 

The dashboard is designed to be simple and easy to use. Each page has a clear, uncluttered layout so users can quickly find and understand what they see. Interactive elements like filters, tooltips, and export options allow users to explore the data at their own pace. The two-page structure ensures that neither user is confronted with charts or filters that are irrelevant to their goals, making the experience more intuitive and trustworthy.


## <span style="color: #009a27;">Project Plan Séverine Stadler</span>

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-project-plan
%%| fig-cap: Preliminary project plan. Milestones are not final.
gantt
    title Project Plan
    dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD
    tickInterval 1 week
    axisFormat %d.%m.%Y

    section Project Initialization
    Decision on Project Idea : milestone, m1, 2026-03-14, 2d
    Project Charta Draft : a1, 2026-03-14, 7d

    section Project Understanding
    Stakeholder and user analysis : a3, 2026-03-18, 8d
    Project objectives and visualization concept : a4, 2026-03-28, 7d  

    section Data Acquisition and Exploration
    Acquire data : a5, 2026-04-01, 2d
    EDA : a6, 2026-04-01, 8d
    Discuss data report : milestone, m2, 2026-04-09, 0d

    section Visual Encoding and Design
    Implement Dashboard prototype : a7, 2026-04-10, 21d
    Complete visualization report : a8, 2026-05-01, 18d

    section Submission
    Draft Presentation Submission : milestone, m3, 2026-05-18, 0d
    Project Submission : milestone, m4, 2026-06-01, 0d

    section Presentation
    Prepare presentation : a10, 2026-05-30, 9d
    Project presentation - exam period : a11, 2026-06-08, 21d
```


## <span style="color: #009a27;">Roles and Contact Details</span>
- Jasmin Fankhauser - Dashboard Visualization and Presentation (fankhjas@students.zhaw.ch)
- Séverine Stadler - Project Charta and Dashboard Visualization (stadlsev@students.zhaw.ch)
- Lia Sifrig - Project Charta and Dashboard Visualization (sifrilia@students.zhaw.ch)
- Anna Kaufmann - Dashboard Visualization and Presentation (kaufma03@students.zhaw.ch)
